title: Path with minimum effort (grid + Dijkstra/binary search).
description: Path with minimum effort (grid + Dijkstra/binary search).

examples.explanation: Start at (0,0)=1, min_effort=∞. Path 1: (0,0)→(0,1): diff=|1-2|=1, max=1; (0,1)→(0,2): diff=|2-2|=0, max=1; (0,2)→(1,2): diff=|2-2|=0, max=1; (1,2)→(2,2): diff=|2-5|=3, max=3; reach end, min_effort=3. Backtrack to explore other paths. Path 2: (0,0)→(1,0): diff=|1-3|=2, max=2; (1,0)→(2,0): diff=|3-5|=2, max=2; (2,0)→(2,1): diff=|5-3|=2, max=2; (2,1)→(2,2): diff=|3-5|=2, max=2; reach end, min_effort=2. Continue exploring other paths but none yield <2. Final min_effort=2.

Start at (0,0)=1. Explore paths systematically. One optimal path: (0,0)→(0,1): diff=|1-2|=1, max=1; (0,1)→(0,2): diff=|2-3|=1, max=1; (0,2)→(1,2): diff=|3-4|=1, max=1; (1,2)→(2,2): diff=|4-5|=1, max=1; reach end, min_effort=1. Other paths have higher max differences (e.g., through 8 gives diff=7). Final min_effort=1.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(4^(m*n))
approaches.summary: Explore all possible paths from start to end using depth-first search, tracking the maximum absolute difference in heights along each path, and return the minimum of these maximums. This is straightforward but extremely slow for larger grids due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS for Minimum Effort Path
ladders.desc: Find the minimum possible maximum absolute height difference along any path from top-left to bottom-right in a grid by exploring all possible paths using DFS.

explanation.prose: This problem requires finding a path from the top-left cell (0,0) to the bottom-right cell (m-1,n-1) in an m×n grid where each cell has an integer height. The 'effort' of a path is defined as the maximum absolute difference in heights between consecutive cells along that path. Our goal is to find the minimum possible effort among all valid paths. The brute force DFS approach systematically explores every possible path from start to end using depth-first search. For each path, we track the maximum height difference encountered so far. When we reach the destination, we update a global minimum effort variable if the current path's maximum difference is smaller. The intuition is that by exploring all possible paths, we're guaranteed to find the optimal solution, but this comes at the cost of exponential time complexity. The algorithm works because it's essentially performing an exhaustive search through the state space of all possible paths. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize min_effort to infinity. 2. Create a visited matrix to track cells in current path. 3. Define DFS function that takes current position and current_max_diff. 4. If at destination, update min_effort with min(min_effort, current_max_diff). 5. Otherwise, for each of the 4 possible directions (up, down, left, right), if the neighbor is within bounds and not visited, calculate the height difference between current and neighbor, update current_max_diff to max(current_max_diff, diff), mark neighbor visited, recurse, then unmark visited (backtracking). 6. Start DFS from (0,0) with current_max_diff = 0.

connectsTo.reason: This full solution relies on the DFS traversal with backtracking as the key subroutine

timeComplexity.note: ) in worst case because at each cell we have up to 4 choices and we might explore all possible paths

spaceComplexity.note: for the recursion stack and visited matrix

commonMistakes: not handling the base case properly (updating min_effort only at destination)
forgetting to backtrack (which would prevent exploring all paths)
incorrectly calculating the maximum difference (should be max of current_max_diff
new difference
not sum)
explanation.codePattern: def dfs(i, j, current_max): if i==m-1 and j==n-1: min_effort = min(min_effort, current_max); return; for each direction: calculate diff = abs(heights[i][j]-heights[ni][nj]); new_max = max(current_max, diff); mark visited; dfs(ni, nj, new_max); unmark visited

examples.trace: Start at (0,0)=1, min_effort=∞. Path 1: (0,0)→(0,1): diff=|1-2|=1, max=1; (0,1)→(0,2): diff=|2-2|=0, max=1; (0,2)→(1,2): diff=|2-2|=0, max=1; (1,2)→(2,2): diff=|2-5|=3, max=3; reach end, min_effort=3. Backtrack to explore other paths. Path 2: (0,0)→(1,0): diff=|1-3|=2, max=2; (1,0)→(2,0): diff=|3-5|=2, max=2; (2,0)→(2,1): diff=|5-3|=2, max=2; (2,1)→(2,2): diff=|3-5|=2, max=2; reach end, min_effort=2. Continue exploring other paths but none yield <2. Final min_effort=2.

Start at (0,0)=1. Explore paths systematically. One optimal path: (0,0)→(0,1): diff=|1-2|=1, max=1; (0,1)→(0,2): diff=|2-3|=1, max=1; (0,2)→(1,2): diff=|3-4|=1, max=1; (1,2)→(2,2): diff=|4-5|=1, max=1; reach end, min_effort=1. Other paths have higher max differences (e.g., through 8 gives diff=7). Final min_effort=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Backtracking for Grid Paths
ladders.desc: Implement depth-first search that explores all possible paths in a grid while tracking visited cells and backtracking to explore alternatives.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: a DFS that systematically explores all paths from a starting cell to a destination cell in a grid. The key insight is using backtracking—marking cells as visited when entering a path branch, then unmarking them when leaving that branch—to ensure all possible paths are explored. Without backtracking, we would only explore one path and miss alternatives. The intuition is that we're building a search tree where each node represents a cell, and edges represent moves to neighbors. By recursively exploring each possible direction and then undoing the visit mark, we ensure the entire tree is traversed. Step-by-step: 1. Define a recursive function dfs(i, j) that takes current coordinates. 2. If (i,j) is the destination, process the result (in full problem, update min effort). 3. Otherwise, mark (i,j) as visited. 4. For each of the 4 directions (up, down, left, right): if neighbor is within bounds and not visited, recursively call dfs on neighbor. 5. After exploring all directions from (i,j), unmark (i,j) as visited (backtracking). Time complexity for this sub-routine alone is O(4^(m*n)) in worst case because each cell can lead to 4 recursive calls. Connection to L2: The backtracking mechanism enables the exhaustive search.

connectsTo.reason: This DFS with backtracking is exactly what's used in the full solution to explore all paths

spaceComplexity.note: for recursion stack and visited matrix

commonMistakes: forgetting to unmark visited cells (leads to incomplete exploration)
not checking bounds before accessing neighbors (causes index errors)
infinite recursion if destination check is missing
explanation.codePattern: visited = [[False]*n for _ in range(m)]; def dfs(i,j): if i==dest_i and j==dest_j: # process; return; visited[i][j]=True; for di,dj in directions: ni,nj=i+di,j+dj; if 0<=ni<m and 0<=nj<n and not visited[ni][nj]: dfs(ni,nj); visited[i][j]=False

examples.input: Grid 2x2, start=(0,0), end=(1,1), find all paths
examples.trace: Start dfs(0,0): mark visited. From (0,0), two neighbors: (0,1) and (1,0). First explore (0,1): dfs(0,1): mark visited. From (0,1), neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1) not visited. dfs(1,1): destination reached, record path. Backtrack: unmark (1,1), return to (0,1), unmark (0,1). Back to (0,0). Now explore (1,0): dfs(1,0): mark visited. From (1,0), neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1) not visited. dfs(1,1): destination reached, record path. Backtrack: unmark (1,1), return to (1,0), unmark (1,0). Back to (0,0), unmark (0,0). Done.

examples.input: Grid 1x3, start=(0,0), end=(0,2), find all paths
examples.trace: Start dfs(0,0): mark visited. Only neighbor is (0,1) (left/right only in 1D). dfs(0,1): mark visited. From (0,1), neighbor (0,2) not visited. dfs(0,2): destination reached, record path. Backtrack: unmark (0,2), return to (0,1), unmark (0,1), return to (0,0), unmark (0,0). Only one path exists.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Maximum Difference Along a Path
ladders.desc: While traversing a path, maintain the maximum absolute height difference between consecutive cells encountered so far.

explanation.prose: This is the central technique in the minimum effort problem: as we traverse a path, we need to track the 'current maximum difference'—the largest absolute difference in heights between any two consecutive cells along the path from start to current position. The intuition is that the effort of a path is defined as this maximum difference, so to evaluate a path, we must compute this value incrementally. At each step moving from cell A to cell B, we calculate diff = |height(A)-height(B)|, then update current_max = max(current_max, diff). This ensures current_max always holds the maximum difference seen so far on this path. This is efficient because it's O(1) per move and avoids storing all differences. The algorithm: 1. Initialize current_max = 0 at start cell (no movement yet). 2. For each move from cell (i,j) to neighbor (ni,nj): diff = abs(heights[i][j] - heights[ni][nj]); current_max = max(current_max, diff). 3. Pass current_max recursively to next steps. Time complexity per path is O(L) where L is path length. Connection to L3: This relies on calculating absolute differences between grid cells.

connectsTo.reason: In the DFS, we pass current_max as a parameter to the recursive function

commonMistakes: resetting current_max incorrectly (should persist along path)
using sum instead of max
or not initializing to 0
explanation.codePattern: In dfs(i,j,current_max): for move to neighbor: new_diff = abs(heights[i][j]-heights[ni][nj]); new_max = max(current_max, new_diff); dfs(ni,nj,new_max)

examples.input: Path: cells with heights [1,3,5,2], track max diff
examples.trace: Start at height 1, current_max=0. Move to 3: diff=|1-3|=2, current_max=max(0,2)=2. Move to 5: diff=|3-5|=2, current_max=max(2,2)=2. Move to 2: diff=|5-2|=3, current_max=max(2,3)=3. Final current_max=3.
Start at 4, current_max=0. Move to 4: diff=|4-4|=0, current_max=max(0,0)=0. Move to 4: diff=0, current_max=0. Move to 4: diff=0, current_max=0. Final current_max=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculating Absolute Differences in a Grid
ladders.desc: Compute the absolute difference between heights of two adjacent cells in a grid (up, down, left, right).

explanation.prose: This intermediate concept involves accessing grid values and computing absolute differences between neighboring cells. In a 2D grid represented as a list of lists, each cell (i,j) has up to 4 neighbors: (i-1,j), (i+1,j), (i,j-1), (i,j+1), provided they are within grid bounds. The absolute difference between two cells is |height1 - height2|, which is always non-negative and represents the effort needed to move between them. The intuition is that this difference quantifies the 'cost' of a move in the path effort problem. Step-by-step: 1. Given grid heights[][] with dimensions m×n. 2. For a move from (i,j) to (ni,nj): first ensure both coordinates are valid (0≤i,ni<m and 0≤j,nj<n). 3. Retrieve values: a = heights[i][j]; b = heights[ni][nj]. 4. Compute diff = |a - b| using absolute value function. Connection to L4: This relies on accessing grid elements by indices.

connectsTo.reason: This difference calculation is used to update the current maximum along a path

commonMistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices (cause runtime error)
incorrect absolute value calculation (e.g
using negative values)
or misunderstanding grid indexing (rows vs columns)
explanation.codePattern: def get_diff(grid, i, j, ni, nj): if 0<=ni<len(grid) and 0<=nj<len(grid[0]): return abs(grid[i][j] - grid[ni][nj]); else: return None (or handle error)

examples.trace: Heights: at (0,0)=1, at (0,1)=2. Compute |1-2| = 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Comparing Grid Elements
ladders.desc: Retrieve values from a 2D grid using row and column indices, and compare two integers.

explanation.prose: This fundamental operation involves working with a 2D grid (matrix) represented as a list of lists in programming. Each inner list represents a row, and elements are accessed using two indices: grid[i][j] gives the value at row i, column j. Row indices typically start at 0 for the top row, and column indices at 0 for the leftmost column. Comparing two integers means using relational operators (<, >, <=, >=, ==, !=) or calculating their difference. The intuition is that grids are a common data structure for problems involving spatial relationships, and accessing elements is basic to any grid algorithm. Step-by-step: 1. Define grid as list of lists, e.g., grid = [[1,2],[3,4]]. 2. Access element at (i,j): value = grid[i][j]. Ensure i and j are within bounds: 0 ≤ i < number of rows, 0 ≤ j < number of columns. 3. Compare two values a and b: e.g., a < b returns True if a is less than b. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L5: This builds on array indexing concepts.

connectsTo.reason: This is used to get heights for difference calculation

commonMistakes: off-by-one errors in indices
confusing rows
columns (grid[i][j] vs grid[j][i])
or accessing empty lists
explanation.codePattern: rows = len(grid); cols = len(grid[0]); if 0<=i<rows and 0<=j<cols: val = grid[i][j]; else: handle error

examples.trace: Grid has 2 rows, 3 columns. Row indices: 0 for [5,10,15], 1 for [20,25,30]. Column indices: 0,1,2. Access grid[1][2]: row 1, column 2 -> value 30.
Access grid[0][0]=1, grid[1][1]=4. Compare: 1 < 4 evaluates to True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand how to store and access elements in a list (array) using indices.

explanation.prose: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: an array (or list in Python) is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in a linear order. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0 for the first element, 1 for the second, and so on. Accessing an element means retrieving its value using its index, e.g., arr[0] gives the first element. The intuition is that arrays allow efficient random access—you can get any element directly if you know its index, in O(1) time. This is fundamental because grids are essentially 2D arrays (lists of lists). Step-by-step: 1. Create a list: arr = [10, 20, 30]. 2. Access element at index i: value = arr[i]. For example, arr[1] returns 20. 3. Indices must be valid: 0 ≤ i < len(arr), where len(arr) gives the number of elements. If index is out of range (e.g., arr[5] for a 3-element list), it causes an error. Time complexity for access is O(1).

connectsTo.reason: 2D grid access extends this to two indices

commonMistakes: using index 1 to get first element (should be
, forgetting that indices start at 0, or accessing beyond the last index (off-by-one)
explanation.codePattern: arr = [5, 10, 15]; first = arr[0]; # 5; second = arr[1]; # 10; length = len(arr); # 3

examples.trace: Array has elements: index 0:3, index 1:7, index 2:1, index 3:9. Access arr[2] returns the element at position 2, which is 1.
Indices: 0->100, 1->200, 2->300. arr[0] returns the first element, 100.

approaches.name: Binary Search with BFS O(m*n*log(H))
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the possible effort values (from 0 to the maximum height difference in the grid) to find the minimum feasible effort. For each mid-value, perform BFS to check if a path exists where all adjacent differences are ≤ mid. This leverages monotonicity in feasibility.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(m*n*log(H)) where H is the range of heights

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Effort Path with Binary Search + BFS
ladders.desc: Find the minimum effort path in a height grid using binary search on effort values with BFS feasibility checks.

explanation.prose: The problem requires finding a path from top-left to bottom-right in an m×n grid where each cell has a height. The effort of a path is defined as the maximum absolute difference in heights between any two consecutive cells along the path. We need to minimize this maximum effort. The binary search approach works because feasibility is monotonic: if a path exists with effort ≤ k, then a path also exists for any effort > k. We binary search over possible effort values from 0 to the maximum height difference in the grid. For each mid-value, we perform BFS to check if we can traverse from (0,0) to (m-1,n-1) while ensuring all adjacent cell differences are ≤ mid. If feasible, we try smaller efforts; otherwise, we try larger efforts. This yields O(m*n*log(H)) time where H is the height range, The intuition leverages that we're searching for the minimum threshold where the grid becomes 'connected' under the effort constraint.

spaceComplexity.note: for BFS queue and visited set

commonMistakes: incorrect binary search bounds (should be 0 to max_height-min_height)
not using visited sets in BFS (causing infinite loops)
mishandling edge cases like 1×1 grids. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the binary search framework with the BFS feasibility check from L1

examples.trace:
1. Grid: [[1,2,2],[3,8,2],[5,3,5]]. Min height=1, max height=8, so H=7. Binary search range [0,7].
2. Mid=3: BFS checks if path exists with effort ≤3. Start at (0,0)=1. Adjacent (0,1)=2 diff=1≤3, (1,0)=3 diff=2≤3. Continue BFS: reach (2,2)=5 from (2,1)=3 diff=2≤3. Path found: (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(1,2)→(2,2). Feasible.
3. Update high=3, mid=1. BFS with effort ≤1: From (0,0)=1, (0,1)=2 diff=1≤1 ok, but (1,1)=8 from (0,1) diff=6>1, and other paths fail. Not feasible.
4. Update low=2, mid=2. BFS with effort ≤2: Similar to step 2 but with tighter constraint. Path (0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,1)→(2,2): diffs: |1-3|=2, |3-5|=2, |5-3|=2, |3-5|=2 all ≤2. Feasible.
5. Binary search ends with low=2. Return 2.

1. Grid: [[1,2,3],[3,8,4],[5,3,5]]. Min=1, max=8, H=7. Range [0,7].
2. Mid=3: BFS with effort ≤3. Start (0,0)=1. Explore: reach (2,2)=5 via path with max diff 2 (e.g., |3-5|=2). Feasible.
3. High=3, mid=1. BFS with effort ≤1: From (0,0)=1, (0,1)=2 diff=1≤1 ok, but (1,1)=8 diff=6>1 blocks. Try path (0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,1)→(2,2): |1-3|=2>1 fails. Not feasible.
4. Low=2, mid=2. BFS with effort ≤2: Path (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(1,2)→(2,2): diffs: |1-2|=1, |2-3|=1, |3-4|=1, |4-5|=1 all ≤2. Feasible.
5. High=2, mid=1 (since low=2, high=2, mid=1? Wait, binary search: after step 3, low=2, high=3, mid=2. After step 4 feasible, set high=2. Now low=2, high=2, exit loop. Return low=2? Let's recalc: Start low=0, high=7. Mid=3 feasible → high=3. Mid=1 not feasible → low=2. Now low=2, high=3, mid=2 feasible → high=2. Now low=2, high=2, exit. Return 2. But expected output is 1? Check grid: path (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(1,2)→(2,2) has diffs: 1,1,1,1 max=1. So effort 1 should be feasible. BFS with effort=1: From (0,0)=1, go to (0,1)=2 diff=1 ok, to (0,2)=3 diff=1 ok, to (1,2)=4 diff=1 ok, to (2,2)=5 diff=1 ok. Yes feasible! So binary search: after mid=1 not feasible? I made error: In step 3, BFS with effort=1: from (0,0)=1, (0,1)=2 diff=1 ok, (0,2)=3 from (0,1) diff=1 ok, (1,2)=4 from (0,2) diff=1 ok, (2,2)=5 from (1,2) diff=1 ok. So it is feasible! Correct trace: 1. Range [0,7], mid=3 feasible → high=3. 2. Mid=1 feasible → high=1. 3. Mid=0 not feasible → low=1. 4. Low=1, high=1, return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Feasibility Check for Given Effort
ladders.desc: Check if a path exists from start to end in a grid with all adjacent height differences ≤ a given effort using BFS.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine determines whether it's possible to traverse from the top-left cell (0,0) to the bottom-right cell (m-1,n-1) in an m×n height grid, given a maximum allowed effort k. The effort for moving between two adjacent cells (up, down, left, right) is the absolute difference in their heights. A path is feasible if all such efforts along the path are ≤ k. We use BFS (or DFS) to explore the grid starting from (0,0), only moving to neighboring cells if the height difference is ≤ k. We maintain a visited set to avoid cycles. If we reach (m-1,n-1), return True; otherwise, False after exploring all reachable cells. The intuition is that BFS systematically checks connectivity under the effort constraint, treating cells as nodes and edges as valid moves.

timeComplexity.note: since each cell is visited at most once, and space is O(m*n) for the queue and visited set

commonMistakes: forgetting to check bounds
not using visited set (causing infinite loops)
incorrect neighbor checks. This feeds into L0 by being the core check within each binary search iteration
explanation.codePattern: Use a queue with (r,c), visited matrix, directions list, and loop while queue not empty

examples.trace:
1. Start at (0,0)=1, visited={(0,0)}.
2. Queue: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0). Check neighbors: (0,1)=2 diff=|1-2|=1≤2 → add (0,1) to queue and visited. (1,0)=3 diff=|1-3|=2≤2 → add (1,0).
3. Queue: [(0,1),(1,0)]. Pop (0,1)=2. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (0,2)=2 diff=0≤2 → add (0,2), (1,1)=8 diff=6>2 skip.
4. Queue: [(1,0),(0,2)]. Pop (1,0)=3. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (2,0)=5 diff=2≤2 → add (2,0), (1,1)=8 diff=5>2 skip.
5. Queue: [(0,2),(2,0)]. Pop (0,2)=2. Neighbors: (0,1) visited, (1,2)=2 diff=0≤2 → add (1,2).
6. Queue: [(2,0),(1,2)]. Pop (2,0)=5. Neighbors: (1,0) visited, (2,1)=3 diff=2≤2 → add (2,1).
7. Queue: [(1,2),(2,1)]. Pop (1,2)=2. Neighbors: (0,2) visited, (2,2)=5 diff=3>2 skip.
8. Queue: [(2,1)]. Pop (2,1)=3. Neighbors: (2,0) visited, (2,2)=5 diff=2≤2 → add (2,2).
9. Queue: [(2,2)]. Pop (2,2)=5, which is the target. Return True.

1. Start at (0,0)=1, visited={(0,0)}.
2. Queue: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0). Check neighbors: (0,1)=2 diff=1>0 skip, (1,0)=3 diff=2>0 skip.
3. Queue empty. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Monotonic Feasibility
ladders.desc: Use binary search to find the minimum effort value where the BFS feasibility check returns true.

explanation.prose: The core insight is that the feasibility function f(k) — which returns True if a path exists with effort ≤ k — is monotonic: if f(k) is True, then f(k') is True for all k' ≥ k. This monotonicity allows us to apply binary search over the range of possible effort values [0, H], where H is the maximum height difference in the grid (max_height - min_height). We initialize low=0 and high=H. While low < high, compute mid = (low + high) // 2. Check f(mid) using the BFS sub-routine. If True, set high = mid (since we want the minimum, and any value ≥ mid might also be feasible). If False, set low = mid + 1 (since efforts ≤ mid are insufficient). After the loop, low (or high) is the minimum feasible effort. The intuition is similar to finding the first True in a sorted boolean array.

timeComplexity.note: iterations, each calling BFS O(m*n), so total O(m*n*log H)

commonMistakes: incorrect initial high (should be H
not a fixed large number)
off-by-one in binary search (use while low < high
not ≤)
not handling the case when low==high. This feeds into L0 by providing the search framework that wraps the BFS check from L1
explanation.codePattern: while low < high: mid = (low+high)//2; if feasible(mid): high = mid; else: low = mid+1; return low

examples.input: feasibility results: f(0)=F, f(1)=F, f(2)=T, f(3)=T, f(4)=T
examples.trace:
1. Range [0,4] (H=4). low=0, high=4.
2. Mid=(0+4)//2=2. f(2)=True → set high=2.
3. Now low=0, high=2. Mid=1. f(1)=False → set low=2.
4. Now low=2, high=2. Exit loop. Return low=2.

1. Range [0,2]. low=0, high=2.
2. Mid=1. f(1)=True → set high=1.
3. Now low=0, high=1. Mid=0. f(0)=True → set high=0.
4. Now low=0, high=0. Exit. Return 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal with BFS
ladders.desc: Traverse a 2D grid from start to end using BFS, moving to adjacent cells.

explanation.prose: This intermediate concept involves traversing an m×n grid from a starting cell (e.g., (0,0)) to a target cell (e.g., (m-1,n-1)) using Breadth-First Search (BFS). BFS explores cells level by level, ensuring the shortest path in terms of number of moves (though here we care about connectivity, not shortest distance). We use a queue (e.g., deque) to store cells to visit, and a visited set (e.g., boolean matrix) to avoid revisiting. Start by enqueueing the start cell and marking it visited. While the queue is not empty, dequeue a cell, check if it's the target, and if so, return success. Otherwise, for each of its four neighbors (up, down, left, right), if within bounds and not visited, enqueue the neighbor and mark visited. If the queue empties without reaching the target, return failure. The intuition is that BFS systematically explores all reachable cells, making it ideal for connectivity checks. Time and space are O(m*n).
commonMistakes: using DFS which might be less efficient for some grids
forgetting to check bounds
or not marking visited when enqueueing (causing duplicates). This feeds into L1 by forming the base traversal that is enhanced with effort constraints
explanation.codePattern: directions = [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]; queue = deque([(0,0)]); visited[0][0] = True; while queue: r,c = queue

examples.trace:
1. Start at (0,0), visited[0][0]=True, queue=[(0,0)].
2. Pop (0,0). Not target. Neighbors: (0,1) and (1,0) within bounds, not visited → add to queue, mark visited. Queue now [(0,1),(1,0)].
3. Pop (0,1). Not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (0,2) and (1,1) not visited → add, mark. Queue now [(1,0),(0,2),(1,1)].
4. Pop (1,0). Not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (2,0) and (1,1) (1,1 already in queue but not visited? Actually visited when added from step 3) → add (2,0) if not visited. Mark visited. Queue now [(0,2),(1,1),(2,0)].
5. Pop (0,2). Not target. Neighbors: (0,1) visited, (1,2) not visited → add, mark. Queue now [(1,1),(2,0),(1,2)].
6. Pop (1,1). Not target. Neighbors: (0,1) visited, (2,1) not visited → add, mark. Queue now [(2,0),(1,2),(2,1)].
7. Pop (2,0). Not target. Neighbors: (1,0) visited, (2,1) already in queue. Queue now [(1,2),(2,1)].
8. Pop (1,2). Not target. Neighbors: (0,2) visited, (2,2) not visited → add, mark. Queue now [(2,1),(2,2)].
9. Pop (2,1). Not target. Neighbors: (1,1) visited, (2,0) visited, (2,2) already in queue. Queue now [(2,2)].
10. Pop (2,2). This is the target. Return True.

1. Start (0,0), visited[0][0]=True, queue=[(0,0)].
2. Pop (0,0). Not target. Neighbors: (0,1) and (1,0) → add, mark. Queue=[(0,1),(1,0)].
3. Pop (0,1). Not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1) → add, mark. Queue=[(1,0),(1,1)].
4. Pop (1,0). Not target. Neighbors: (0,0) visited, (1,1) already in queue. Queue=[(1,1)].
5. Pop (1,1). Target. Return True.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Adjacent Cell Validity in Grid
ladders.desc: Given a cell in a 2D grid, check if moving to a neighboring cell is valid based on bounds and conditions.

explanation.prose: This fundamental operation involves checking whether moving from a current cell (r,c) to a neighbor cell (nr,nc) in a grid is valid. The check has two parts: 1) Bounds check: ensure nr and nc are within the grid dimensions (0 ≤ nr < m and 0 ≤ nc < n). 2) Condition check: based on the problem, such as checking if the height difference between grid[r][c] and grid[nr][nc] is ≤ a given effort k. For this sub-problem, we focus on the bounds check and a generic condition (e.g., always true for simple traversal). The intuition is that grid traversal requires validating each move to avoid index errors and apply problem-specific constraints. This operation is performed for each of the four directions (up, down, left, right).
commonMistakes: off-by-one errors in bounds (e.g
using ≤ instead of <)
forgetting to check both row
column bounds
not handling edge cells properly. This feeds into L3 by being the core step in BFS when exploring neighbors
explanation.codePattern: directions = [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]; for dr,dc in directions: nr, nc = r+dr, c+dc; if 0 <= nr < m and 0 <= nc < n and condition(grid[r][c], grid[nr][nc]): # proceed

examples.input: grid = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]], current=(0,0), direction=(0,1) (right), condition: always true
examples.trace:
1. Current cell (0,0). Neighbor (0,1) by adding direction (0,1).
2. Check bounds: nr=0, nc=1. Grid has m=2 rows, n=3 columns. 0≤0<2 True, 0≤1<3 True.
3. Condition is always true.
4. Move is valid.

examples.input: grid = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]], current=(1,2), direction=(1,0) (down), condition: always true
examples.trace:
1. Current (1,2). Neighbor (2,2) by adding (1,0).
2. Check bounds: nr=2, nc=2. m=2, so 0≤2<2 is False (2 is not less than 2).
3. Move invalid due to out of bounds.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Absolute Difference and Comparison
ladders.desc: Compute the absolute difference between two numbers and compare it to a threshold.

explanation.prose: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: given two integers a and b, compute their absolute difference |a - b|, and check if this difference is less than or equal to a threshold value k. The absolute difference is the positive distance between the numbers, calculated as a-b if a ≥ b, or b-a if b > a. In programming, use abs(a-b). Then, compare abs(a-b) ≤ k. If true, the difference is within the allowed effort; if false, it exceeds the effort. The intuition is that in the path effort problem, moving between cells requires the height difference to be within a limit to ensure the path is feasible. This operation is fundamental to the condition check in L4.
commonMistakes: forgetting to use abs (leading to negative differences)
incorrect comparison operators (e.g
using < instead of ≤)
not understanding that effort is defined as the maximum difference along the path. This feeds into L4 by providing the specific condition for grid traversal
explanation.codePattern: if abs(grid[r][c] - grid[nr][nc]) <= k: # move is allowed

examples.trace:
1. Compute absolute difference: |5-3| = 2.
2. Compare to k=2: 2 ≤ 2 is True.
3. So difference is within threshold.

1. Absolute difference: |10-1| = 9.
2. Compare to k=5: 9 ≤ 5 is False.
3. Difference exceeds threshold.

approaches.name: Dijkstra's Algorithm O(m*n*log(m*n))
approaches.summary: Treat the grid as a graph where nodes are cells and edge weights are the absolute differences in heights between adjacent cells. Use Dijkstra's algorithm with a priority queue to find the path from start to end that minimizes the maximum edge weight along the path. This directly computes the minimum effort without guessing.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Effort Path with Dijkstra's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding path from top-left to bottom-right that minimizes maximum absolute height difference between adjacent cells.

explanation.prose: This problem asks us to find a path through a grid where the 'effort' is defined as the maximum absolute height difference between any two adjacent cells along the path. We need to minimize this maximum effort. The key insight is that we can treat this as a shortest path problem in a graph where nodes are grid cells and edge weights are the absolute differences between adjacent cell heights. However, instead of minimizing the sum of edge weights (traditional Dijkstra), we need to minimize the maximum edge weight along any path. This is a minimax path problem. We can adapt Dijkstra's algorithm by modifying the relaxation step: when considering moving from cell A to cell B, the 'cost' to reach B is the maximum of (1) the current maximum effort to reach A, and (2) the absolute difference between heights of A and B. We use a priority queue (min-heap) to always expand the node with the smallest current maximum effort. The algorithm starts at (0,0) with effort 0, processes neighbors, updates efforts if we find a smaller maximum effort path, and continues until we reach (m-1,n-1). The .

timeComplexity.note: ) because each cell can be pushed/popped from the priority queue, and space is O(m*n) for the distance array and priority queue

commonMistakes: using traditional Dijkstra that sums weights instead of tracking maximums
not handling the priority queue correctly (forgetting to push updated efforts)

or using BFS which doesn't guarantee optimality for this minimax problem. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete Dijkstra adaptation with the modified relaxation logic

examples.trace: Initialize: efforts array with INF except start (0,0)=0. PQ: [(0,0,0)]. Process (0,0): height=1. Check neighbor (0,1): height=2, diff=|1-2|=1, new_effort=max(0,1)=1 < INF, update efforts[0][1]=1, push (1,0,1). Check (1,0): height=3, diff=2, new_effort=2 < INF, update efforts[1][0]=2, push (2,1,0). PQ now: [(1,0,1),(2,1,0)]. Process (0,1): height=2. Check (0,2): height=2, diff=0, new_effort=max(1,0)=1 < INF, update efforts[0][2]=1, push (1,0,2). Check (1,1): height=8, diff=6, new_effort=6 < INF, update efforts[1][1]=6, push (6,1,1). PQ: [(1,0,2),(2,1,0),(6,1,1)]. Process (0,2): height=2. Check (1,2): height=2, diff=0, new_effort=max(1,0)=1 < INF, update efforts[1][2]=1, push (1,1,2). PQ: [(1,1,2),(2,1,0),(6,1,1)]. Process (1,2): height=2. Check (2,2): height=5, diff=3, new_effort=max(1,3)=3 < INF, update efforts[2][2]=3, push (3,2,2). PQ: [(2,1,0),(3,2,2),(6,1,1)]. Process (1,0): height=3. Check (2,0): height=5, diff=2, new_effort=max(2,2)=2 < INF, update efforts[2][0]=2, push (2,2,0). Check (1,1): height=8, diff=5, new_effort=max(2,5)=5 < 6, update efforts[1][1]=5, push (5,1,1). PQ: [(2,2,0),(2,2,2),(3,2,2),(5,1,1),(6,1,1)]. Process (2,0): height=5. Check (2,1): height=3, diff=2, new_effort=max(2,2)=2 < INF, update efforts[2][1]=2, push (2,2,1). PQ: [(2,2,1),(2,2,2),(3,2,2),(5,1,1),(6,1,1)]. Process (2,1): height=3. Check (2,2): height=5, diff=2, new_effort=max(2,2)=2 < 3, update efforts[2][2]=2, push (2,2,2). Now efforts[2][2]=2. Continue processing but target reached with effort 2. Return 2.

Initialize: efforts all INF except (0,0)=0. PQ: [(0,0,0)]. Process (0,0): height=1. Neighbors: (0,1): diff=1, new_effort=1, update. (1,0): diff=2, new_effort=2, update. PQ: [(1,0,1),(2,1,0)]. Process (0,1): height=2. (0,2): diff=1, new_effort=max(1,1)=1, update efforts[0][2]=1. (1,1): diff=6, new_effort=6, update. PQ: [(1,0,2),(2,1,0),(6,1,1)]. Process (0,2): height=3. (1,2): diff=1, new_effort=max(1,1)=1, update efforts[1][2]=1. PQ: [(1,1,2),(2,1,0),(6,1,1)]. Process (1,2): height=4. (2,2): diff=1, new_effort=max(1,1)=1, update efforts[2][2]=1. Target reached with effort 1. Return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Modified Dijkstra Relaxation for Minimax Path
ladders.desc: Implementing the core relaxation step that updates maximum effort when moving between cells.

explanation.prose: In traditional Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest paths, when we move from node u to node v with edge weight w, we update the distance to v if distance[u] + w < distance[v]. However, for our minimax path problem where we want to minimize the maximum edge weight along the path, we need a different relaxation rule. The key insight is that the effort to reach cell v via cell u is the maximum of two values: (1) the current maximum effort to reach u (which represents the worst height difference encountered so far on that path), and (2) the absolute height difference between cells u and v (which is the potential new maximum if this edge has a larger difference). So new_effort = max(effort[u], abs(height[u] - height[v])). We update effort[v] if new_effort < effort[v]. This relaxation ensures we always track the minimum possible maximum edge weight to reach each cell. The algorithm maintains a priority queue (min-heap) where we process cells in order of their current minimum maximum effort. Each time we pop a cell, we examine its four neighbors (up, down, left, right), compute new_effort using the formula above, and if it improves the neighbor's effort, we update it and push the neighbor to the priority queue. This sub-routine is the heart of the full solution. Time complexity for processing one cell is O(1) for the relaxation checks (constant 4 neighbors), but overall it integrates into the O(m*n*log(m*n)) algorithm.
commonMistakes: using sum instead of max
